schools.summary: The majestic opening (a finely-wrought Greek sentence): 'God... spake (lalesas) at sundry times (polymeros — in many parts, by degrees, piecemeal) and in divers manners (polytropos — in many ways: by vision, dream, type, voice)' — the old revelation coming fragmentarily and variously, not all at once; 'unto the fathers (the patriarchs and people of Israel) by (in, through) the prophets' — the long line of inspired men through whom God revealed himself across the ages, the old covenant's mode of revelation (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: the very manyness and variety of the old revelation, given piecemeal through many prophets, sets up the contrast with the one full and final word now spoken in the Son (v.2); the fragments prepared for the whole.

themes: God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets
God speaking of old in many ways by the prophets
the fragmentary old revelation
prepared for the word in the Son

schools.summary: The contrast with the old revelation: 'hath in these last days (the close of the ages, the Messianic age) spoken unto us by (in) his Son' — over against the many prophets, God's final, complete word spoken in one, his Son, who is himself the revelation; 'whom he hath appointed heir (kleronomon — lord and possessor by right) of all things' — the Son constituted heir and possessor of the whole universe; 'by (through) whom also he made the worlds (tous aionas — the ages, the universe of time and space)' — the Son the agent of creation, through whom God made all things (cf. John 1:3; Colossians 1:16) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Cyril: the Son is both heir of all and maker of the worlds; through him the Father created the ages, a witness to the Son's eternal deity and his agency in creation, distinguishing him utterly from the prophets and angels.

themes: Hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son
whom he hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also he made the worlds
God's final word in his Son
the Son heir and maker of all

schools.summary: The two great terms of the Son's deity: 'the brightness (apaugasma — radiance, effulgence, the shining-forth) of his glory, and the express image (charakter — the exact impress, the stamp as of a seal) of his person (hypostasis — his very substance, being)' — the Son the radiance of God's glory and the exact imprint of his substance, true God of true God; 'upholding (pheron — bearing, carrying, sustaining) all things by the word of his power' — he not only made but sustains the universe, providence as well as creation his; 'when he had by himself purged (made purification for) our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high' — atonement accomplished by his own sacrifice and crowned by his royal session (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom (with Cyril): the Son, being the radiance and exact image of the Father, is of one substance with him; having by himself purged our sins he sat down enthroned — his finished priestly work and his divine glory held together, against all who would lower him to a creature.

themes: the brightness of his glory, and the express image of his person, and upholding all things by the word of his power
when he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high
the Son the radiance of God and exact image
upholder, purger of sins, enthroned

schools.summary: The transition to the great argument of the chapter: 'being made (genomenos — having become, in his exaltation) so much better (kreitton — superior, more excellent) than the angels' — the exalted Son far superior to the angels, this superiority now to be proved from Scripture; 'being made better' referring not to his eternal nature (in which he was always superior) but to his exaltation as Mediator; 'as (in proportion as) he hath by inheritance obtained (inherited) a more excellent name (the name Son) than they' — the name 'Son,' belonging to him by inheritance, marking his superiority, the angels being servants and he the Son (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Being made so much better than the angels, as he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they
the Son made better than the angels
superior by his more excellent name
the name Son above the angels

schools.summary: The Son's superiority rests on the name 'Son' never given to any single angel: 'Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee' (Psalm 2:7) and 'I will be to him a Father, and he shall be to me a Son' (2 Samuel 7:14) — names of unique sonship given to the Messiah. Barnes, with Clarke and Calvin, refer 'this day have I begotten thee' to the resurrection and exaltation: Acts 13:33 applies the verse so, Christ being 'the firstbegotten of the dead' (Revelation 1:5; Romans 1:4); Theodoret: 'it does not express his eternal generation, but that which is connected with time.' Calvin expressly rejects the eternal-generation reading here ('there is nothing to countenance such a view'), taking the 'today' of Christ the antitype as his resurrection and exaltation to God's right hand; Clarke notes it may refer to the incarnation or (most properly) the resurrection.

Wesley: 'I have begotten thee from eternity, which by its unalterable permanency of duration is one continued, unsuccessive day' — the eternal, inconceivable generation; the former clause relating to his natural Sonship by eternal generation, the latter to the Father's acknowledgment of him as incarnate Son. Ellicott points likewise to the 'eternal Now,' the day co-extensive with the everlasting life of God, while granting the resurrection 'day' is itself typical of it.

Cyril: the prophets affirm 'the Lord said unto me, Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee' of the Messiah, never of an angel — proving him more than a creature, the true Son through whom alone men come to the Father.

debates.question: To what does 'this day have I begotten thee' refer?

sides.position: The resurrection and exaltation (Acts 13:33), when God publicly declared and constituted him Son
The eternal generation of the Son (the 'eternal Now')
The incarnation (a view noted by Clarke)

themes: Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee
I will be to him a Father, and he shall be to me a Son
the Son's unique name proved from Scripture
the crux of the begetting

schools.summary: The further proof: 'when he bringeth in (introduces) the firstbegotten (prototokon — the firstborn, supreme in rank and dignity, not first in time) into the world' — God's bringing of his firstborn Son into the world (interpreters differing whether the incarnation/first advent or the second coming, but most taking it of the Son's entrance); 'and let all the angels of God worship (proskyneo — bow down, adore) him' (Deuteronomy 32:43 LXX / Psalm 97:7) — God commanding the angels to worship the Son, the very beings to whom he is compared bidden to adore him, proving his infinite superiority (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): if the angels are commanded to worship the Son, he is no creature but God; worship belongs to God alone, and the Son receives it — a decisive proof of his deity against those who would rank him among the angels.

themes: when he bringeth in the firstbegotten into the world
let all the angels of God worship him
the angels commanded to worship the Son
worship proving the Son's deity

schools.summary: The citation (Psalm 104:4) showing the angels' subordinate, mutable nature: 'of the angels he saith, Who maketh his angels spirits (pneumata — winds, or spirits), and his ministers a flame of fire' — the angels God's servants, likened to winds and flames, swift and changeable instruments of his will, created ministering forces (whether 'spirits' or 'winds,' the point is their service and mutability); they are made (created) instruments, not enthroned sons, over against the Son who is worshipped and enthroned (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Who maketh his angels spirits, and his ministers a flame of fire
the angels as servants, made winds and flame
created, changeable instruments
the contrast with the enthroned Son

schools.summary: The words of Psalm 45:6-7 are applied to the Son. Barnes, with Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley, and Cyril, take them as a direct address to the Son as God (the vocative ho theos): 'Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever' — the Son called God, his throne eternal, his sceptre one of righteousness (a just, equitable reign). Barnes, Clarke, and Calvin defend the vocative against the Unitarian rendering 'God is thy throne,' arguing the whole scope, the Hebrew (kisaka Elohim), and the ancient versions (Aquila's Thee, the Arabic vocative, Wycliffe, Coverdale, Tyndale) require it; the eternity of the throne ('for ever and ever') proving his deity, for whose throne can be established for ever but God's? Robertson and Ellicott note the alternative 'God is thy throne' (ho theos as nominative, which the variant reading 'his kingdom' might support) is grammatically possible — Robertson: 'either makes good sense' — but Ellicott grants the vocative has 'the support of almost all ancient authority.'

Calvin: 'whosoever will read the verse, who is of a sound mind and free from the spirit of contention, cannot doubt but that the Messiah is called God'; the Jews' evasions (making Elohim govern 'throne,' or supplying 'established') are 'mere evasions,' for Elohim is never given simply to one person except God alone, and the perpetuity of his throne is itself an evidence of his divinity.

debates.question: Is 'Thy throne, O God' a direct address to the Son as God, or 'God is thy throne'?

sides.position: A direct address — the Son is called God (the vocative), a proof of his deity
'God is thy throne' (ho theos as nominative) — grammatically possible, and suiting the variant 'his kingdom'
holders: Ellicott (notes it, but favours the vocative on ancient authority)

themes: Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom
the Son addressed as God on an eternal throne
the deity crux: O God or God is thy throne
the eternal, righteous reign

schools.summary: The ground of his anointing: 'thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity' — the Son's perfect love of right and hatred of wrong, his moral excellence the reason for his exaltation; 'therefore (because of this) God, even thy God, hath anointed (echrisen — consecrated, exalted) thee with the oil of gladness above thy fellows (companions)' — God anointing the Son with joy and honour beyond all others, the 'oil of gladness' the symbol of joyful exaltation and consecration (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ was anointed not with material oil but with the Holy Ghost (the oil of gladness, because he is the author of spiritual gladness), above his fellows; the Father, having appointed him Saviour of the world, anointed him with the Spirit — and believers are made partakers and fellows of Christ in their own anointing.

themes: Thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity; therefore God, even thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness above thy fellows
the Son anointed with gladness above his fellows
exalted for his love of righteousness
anointed with the Spirit, the oil of gladness

schools.summary: The further citation (Psalm 102:25-27), applied to the Son: 'Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth; and the heavens are the works of thine hands' — the Son addressed as 'Lord' and as the Creator who laid the earth's foundation in the beginning and made the heavens; words spoken of Jehovah the Creator in the Psalm here applied to the Son, ascribing to him the full work of creation — a decisive proof of his true deity (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril (with the Reformed): that words addressed in the Psalm to Jehovah the eternal Creator are applied to the Son shows him to be the eternal Lord who made heaven and earth — no creature, but the Maker of all, of one being with the Father.

themes: Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth; and the heavens are the works of thine hands
the Son addressed as the eternal Creator
words of Jehovah applied to the Son
the Maker of heaven and earth

schools.summary: The contrast between the creation and the Creator: 'they (the heavens and earth) shall perish (pass away, be dissolved); but thou remainest (continuest, abidest)' — the created universe perishable, but the Son enduring; 'and they all shall wax old (grow old, decay) as doth a garment' — the heavens and earth, like a worn-out garment, growing old and decaying, the whole creation aging and wearing out, over against the Son who abides unchanged (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the perishing of the creation over against the abiding of the Son proves his eternity and immutability; he who outlasts and outlives the universe he made is God, not a creature subject to decay.

themes: They shall perish; but thou remainest; and they all shall wax old as doth a garment
the creation perishing while the Son remains
the changing creation and the unchanging Creator
the Son's eternity proved

schools.summary: The figure completed: 'and as a vesture (garment, mantle) shalt thou fold them up (helixeis — roll them up, as one rolls up a worn cloak), and they shall be changed (transformed, made new)' — the Son folding up the heavens like a worn garment and changing them, the creation mutable in his hands; 'but thou art the same (ho autos — the unchanging One)' — the immutability of God ascribed to the Son; 'and thy years shall not fail (not come to an end, not be exhausted)' — the Son's existence without end, his years eternal, the very attributes of God (immutability and eternity) belonging to him (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the Son is 'the same,' unchanging, and his years fail not — eternal and immutable; the attributes that belong to God alone are ascribed to him, proving him the eternal Lord above all the changing creation.

themes: as a vesture shalt thou fold them up, and they shall be changed: but thou art the same, and thy years shall not fail
the Son the same for ever while the heavens are changed
the immutability and eternity of the Son
God's attributes ascribed to the Son

schools.summary: The closing citation (Psalm 110:1): 'but to which of the angels said he at any time, Sit on my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool?' — the place of supreme honour and authority, at God's right hand, given to the Son and never to any angel (the rhetorical question expecting the answer 'none'); 'until I make thine enemies thy footstool' (the figure of complete subjugation, a conqueror placing his foot on the necks of the vanquished) — all the Son's enemies subdued under his feet, his reign continuing till total victory (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the Son sits enthroned at God's right hand, ruling until all foes are subdued — an honour and dominion never given to any angel, who are but ministering servants; the contrast is complete and decisive.

themes: to which of the angels said he at any time, Sit on my right hand
until I make thine enemies thy footstool
the Son enthroned at God's right hand, never an angel
the place of supreme honour

schools.summary: The summary of the angels' true rank: 'are they not all ministering spirits (leitourgika pneumata — serving spirits, spirits employed in service)?' — the rhetorical question affirming that all the angels are servants, their office ministry not dominion; 'sent forth (dispatched, commissioned) to minister (diakonian — to render service) for them who shall be heirs of salvation (the redeemed, those who will inherit salvation)' — the angels sent out by God on errands of service to and for believers, God's messengers and helpers, not enthroned sons, over against the Son who is served and worshipped as God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for them who shall be heirs of salvation?
the angels as ministering spirits serving the heirs of salvation
servants, not the enthroned Son
the angels minister to the saints
